question: An adult man presents with acute visual symptoms, progressive gait impairment, and new urinary retention. CSF testing including multiple infectious PCRs was nondiagnostic. Spinal MRI is provided. Based on the clinical presentation and the imaging, which initial pharmacologic therapy is most appropriate?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the images. The sagittal spinal MRI shows a long, centrally located T2-hyperintense lesion extending over many vertebral levels (in the cervical to upper thoracic cord), consistent with a longitudinally extensive myelopathy. The chest radiograph shows bilateral patchy/reticulonodular air‑space opacities more prominent centrally and basally.

Next, I relate those findings to the clinical presentation. The patient has acute visual symptoms together with progressive gait impairment and new urinary retention, which localize to the spinal cord with additional optic pathway involvement — a pattern most suggestive of an acute inflammatory CNS process affecting multiple neuroanatomic sites. CSF testing including multiple infectious PCRs was nondiagnostic, so there is no microbiologic confirmation of a specific infectious cause from the CSF.

Then I consider how the answer choices line up with these observations. Intravenous high‑dose methylprednisolone targets acute inflammatory myelopathies and is the appropriate immediate therapy when imaging shows a longitudinal, extensive inflammatory-appearing cord lesion with severe neurologic deficits. High‑dose oral corticosteroids are less appropriate for an acute severe presentation compared with IV therapy. Therapeutic plasma exchange is typically reserved if there is inadequate response to initial immunotherapy or for specific antibody‑mediated syndromes, not usually the first step. Empiric broad‑spectrum antibiotics or anti‑tuberculosis therapy are not supported by the nondiagnostic CSF and by the available imaging alone; the chest x‑ray abnormalities do not provide definitive microbiologic evidence to prioritize those therapies before addressing the acute inflammatory myelopathy.

In summary, the imaging and clinical pattern favor an acute inflammatory myelopathy with severe deficits and nondiagnostic infectious testing, so initial treatment with intravenous high‑dose methylprednisolone is most appropriate.
caption: (a) Spinal cord MRI (sagittal T2 sequence) showed a longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis extending from C5 to T10 (red arrow). (b) Posteroanterior chest radiograph showed bilateral micronodular infiltrate without peripheral consolidation or pleural effusion.
sub_caption: (a) Spinal cord MRI (sagittal T2 sequence) showed a longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis extending from C5 to T10 (red arrow).
image_context: We report the case of a 28-year-old man with no medical history. He presented to the emergency room complaining of ocular pain, blurred vision, gait disorders and dysuria. He also sought care for respiratory symptoms including cough with a small amount of haemoptysis, fever and a decrease in general condition over the preceding few months. At admission, he developed complete urinary retention and a spastic paraplegia. Brain and spinal cord MRI showed a transverse myelitis extending from C5 to T10 (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>a) with optic nerve and optic chiasma contrast recording. Ophthalmological examination revealed the presence of bilateral papilloedema. Visual evoked potential indicated a significant prolongation of the latency of the P100 wave. Lumbar puncture showed clear cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) with albumin cytological dissociation (white blood cell count of 7/mma) with optic nerve and optic chiasma contrast recording. Ophthalmological examination revealed the presence of bilateral papilloedema. Visual evoked potential indicated a significant prolongation of the latency of the P100 wave. Lumbar puncture showed clear cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) with albumin cytological dissociation (white blood cell count of 7/mm3 with elevated protein concentration of 3.23 g/L) and low glycorrhachia (ratio of 1/3). Direct examination and CSF culture were negative. In addition, PCR analysis of the CSF was negative for herpes simplex virus, varicella zoster virus, enteroviruses, Listeria monocytogenes and Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Molecular detection was carried out using the FilmArray® Meningitis/Encephalitis (M/E) Panel from bioMérieux (Marcy l’Étoile, France) and by conventional tests.Fig. 1(a) Spinal cord MRI (sagittal T2 sequence) showed a longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis extending from C5 to T10 (red arrow). (b) Posteroanterior chest radiograph showed bilateral micronodular infiltrate without peripheral consolidation or pleural effusion.Fig. 1 Chest X-ray showed a bilateral micronodular infiltrate (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>b). A thoracic CT revealed bilateral acinar nodes more prevalent in the upper left lobe, associated with several cavitating lesions.b). A thoracic CT revealed bilateral acinar nodes more prevalent in the upper left lobe, associated with several cavitating lesions.

He also sought care for respiratory symptoms including cough with a small amount of haemoptysis, fever and a decrease in general condition over the preceding few months. At admission, he developed complete urinary retention and a spastic paraplegia. Ophthalmological examination revealed the presence of bilateral papilloedema. Visual evoked potential indicated a significant prolongation of the latency of the P100 wave. Lumbar puncture showed clear cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) with albumin cytological dissociation (white blood cell count of 7/mm3 with elevated protein concentration of 3.23 g/L) and low glycorrhachia (ratio of 1/3). Direct examination and CSF culture were negative. In addition, PCR analysis of the CSF was negative for herpes simplex virus, varicella zoster virus, enteroviruses, Listeria monocytogenes and Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
